paragraphs.text: BROWSER CHOICE ALLIANCE
Ref. Confidential 24 October 2025

The Browser Choice Alliance ("BCA") is a coalition of browser providers dedicated to promoting genuine competition on Windows devices for the benefit of consumers and developers alike. Launched in November 2024 to promote the principle that consumers should be free to use their browser of choice on Windows, it is an open and growing alliance whose members already include BrowserWorks, Google Chrome, Midori, Opera, Vivaldi and Wavebox.

This submission is BCA's contribution to the Commission's Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act ("DFA"). In recent years, the European Union has adopted several legislative tools whose objectives, in the BCA's view, would overlap with the ones described in the DFA call for evidence.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Although the BCA does not deem the adoption of a new legislative instrument necessary, in order to provide a constructive view should the Commission still believe otherwise, the BCA would like to highlight some important issues that the Commission should keep in mind. Many of these issues are aligned with the problems affecting consumers that are outlined in the DFA's Call for Evidence (for instance, dark patterns).

evidence_quotes.quote: Misleading design practices, such as limited user choice and dark patterns in various digital products,

paragraphs.text: Overall, we recognise that consumer choice is crucial for digital fairness and competition in the EU. Misleading design practices, such as limited user choice and dark patterns in various digital products, undermine consumers' ability to make informed decisions. It is essential that consumers can set and retain preferred default services, promoting interoperability and switching between platforms, and applying freedom of choice consistently across all device types where competing services are available.

evidence_quotes.quote: persistent issues related to browser competition and user choice on PC devices

paragraphs.text: If the Commission moves forward with the DFA, the Browser Choice Alliance suggests focusing part of its scope on the persistent issues related to browser competition and user choice on PC devices, which currently represent a significant enforcement gap.

Despite not being necessary if existing tools are interpreted and applied both effectively and in full, an eventual DFA could bring added value by providing clarity on the importance of user choice in PC browsers.

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: The BCA has criticised digital enforcement to date as narrowly focused on mobile platforms (i.e., mobile operating systems ("OS") and browsers). However, it is essential that any potential legislative framework concerning digital markets emphasizes the importance of PCs, a key platform used by many millions of European consumers and businesses

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: BROWSER CHOICE ALLIANCE

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: Confidential 24 October 2025

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: everyday. PCs remain a critical and pervasive channel for consumers to access online content and perform computing tasks both in Europe and worldwide. Smartphones and tablets may be convenient for on-the-go content access, but PCs remain essential for more demanding tasks. PCs offer the ability to use larger, multi-monitor displays, precise input tools (like keyboards and mice), more powerful processors, greater storage, and better support for multitasking and prolonged use. Unlike mobile devices, PCs can be upgraded or customised, and their OSs are not interchangeable with those of mobile devices, reflecting the distinct purposes each is designed to serve.

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: All of this makes PCs irreplaceable as tools for enterprises, as well as in the public sector and educational settings. PCs are more suitable for certain consumer use cases too, such as working with documents for non-work matters (e.g., personal admin and finance), extended research for tasks such as planning vacations or researching products, or for longer sessions playing video games or for PC gaming specifically. The typical office worker spends several hours a day using a PC to access the internet. This is borne out by the fact that in many EU countries, users aged over 16 spend as much or more time using computers to access the internet than mobiles.

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: It is estimated that, in Europe, Microsoft, with its ubiquitous Windows PC OS, continues to have by far the leading market share percentage in PC OSs, with a share of over 70%, and several times that of the next largest PC OS supplier. For decades, Microsoft has consistently leveraged its immense market power in Windows and productivity and collaboration software (e.g., Microsoft 365) to award itself an unearned advantage in the browser space, first with Internet Explorer and now with Edge, engaging in conduct on an international scale that harms competition and limits consumer choice. Microsoft's market power, platform ecosystem and conduct has raised antitrust concerns with agencies around the world. In this regard, the Bundeskartellamt recently found that Microsoft is an

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: BROWSER CHOICE ALLIANCE

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: Confidential 24 October 2025

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

paragraphs.text: undertaking of paramount significance for competition across markets. $^{7}$ The publicly available case summary notes in particular that "Microsoft has succeeded in establishing a kind of market standard which users perceive as almost impossible to avoid. With its extensive portfolio and its control of essential layers of the IT infrastructure, Microsoft shapes large parts of the IT sector and, in a comprehensive sense, offers a structure to which other providers must adapt." $^{8}$

section_path: II. Ensuring fair competition and choice for browsers on PC is important for the Single Market

evidence_quotes.quote: used a variety of shifting tactics to build a moat around Edge and obstruct consumers from reliably downloading, setting as default, and using their preferred browsers on Windows PCs

paragraphs.text: It is important to understand that Microsoft has already tipped the playing field in its own favour. Because of Microsoft's licensing and rebate practices, alternative browsers are rarely – if ever – preinstalled on Windows PCs. Freedom of choice on Windows PC OS is therefore critical. Yet, Microsoft has, for years, used a variety of shifting tactics to build a moat around Edge and obstruct consumers from reliably downloading, setting as default, and using their preferred browsers on Windows PCs to access the web at work, school, and home.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: The negative effects on consumers of dark patterns and unfair techniques to pressure, deceive and manipulate consumers is one of the stated areas that the DFA would aim to tackle pursuant to the Call for Evidence (Section B).

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns on Windows PCs

paragraphs.text: Tackling dark patterns on Windows PCs is therefore crucial to effectively allow consumers to make informed and free choices as to their preferred browser and for their choices to be consistently respected. Through such dark patterns, firms like Microsoft have been able to increase, preserve and extend their market power, by pushing users towards their own services and limiting freedom of choice.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns and deceptive practices

paragraphs.text: In the EU today, Microsoft still subjects users to many dark patterns and deceptive practices that restrict competition, harm consumers and hinder their ability to make effective choices. On Windows OS, dark patterns include:

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading banners and pop-ups within Edge and Bing when users search for and try to download rival browsers

paragraphs.text: Tactics that dissuade consumers from completing a product download or acquisition, e.g., misleading banners and pop-ups within Edge and Bing when users search for and try to download rival browsers, encouraging users to stick with Edge (see Annex 1, Figures 1 and 2);9

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: Sludge tactics that make the exercise of user choice subject to excessive steps and steering

paragraphs.text: Sludge tactics that make the exercise of user choice subject to excessive steps and steering, e.g., creating separate default switches within must-have Microsoft apps such as Teams and Outlook that are pre-set to Edge, and deploying misleading messaging within these apps to steer users back to Edge if they try to change this setting (See Annex 1, Figure 3);

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

paragraphs.text: BROWSER CHOICE ALLIANCE

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

paragraphs.text: Confidential 24 October 2025

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: has made it impossible to switch away from Edge as the default browser
severely impedes or blocks entirely the download of rival browsers
displays disproportionate and misleading “scare” banners when a user is considering exiting S mode

paragraphs.text: Erecting technological barriers and deploying unreasonable messaging that raise doubts for users about their choice that are not related to objectively verifiable risks, e.g., on PCs running in S mode – a special configuration of the Windows PC OS which Microsoft claims to be optimised for security and that is the out-of-the-box configuration for millions of Windows PCs that are shipped every year (particularly in the education sector) – Microsoft has made it impossible to switch away from Edge as the default browser (see Annex 1, Figure 4). Furthermore, Microsoft severely impedes or blocks entirely the download of rival browsers and displays disproportionate and misleading “scare” banners when a user is considering exiting S mode, discouraging the user from leaving the highly restrictive and Microsoft-controlled environment (see Annex 1, Figure 4).

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: "exhaust" users into giving up and eventually settling for the dominant OS supplier's browser rather than the browser of their choice.

paragraphs.text: Individually and together, these practices create disjointed user experiences and "exhaust" users into giving up and eventually settling for the dominant OS supplier's browser rather than the browser of their choice.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: dark pattern tactics

paragraphs.text: While existing consumer protection, competition and digital regulation (such as the Digital Markets Act) can, and should, be invoked to address these dark pattern tactics, Microsoft's compliance remains belated, reluctant and partial. Effective enforcement of existing regulations should suffice to tackle this challenge. However, if the DFA is eventually adopted, this should become a priority area.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

paragraphs.text: The BCA considers that, while the issues identified by the Commission are highly relevant, the adoption of the DFA would not be guaranteed. The existing and recent legislative tools, provided that they are effectively implemented, would suffice to achieve the objectives set out in the Call for Evidence, and the BCA wholeheartedly supports the Commission in its diligent enforcement through those tools.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

paragraphs.text: In the event that the Commission still believes necessary to put in place a new legislative framework to address these challenges, the BCA asks the Commission to bear in mind the following considerations:

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

items.topic: Regulatory Parity Between Mobile OS And PC OS

evidence_quotes.quote: It would be nonsensical and a missed opportunity to allow a regulatory gap to emerge whereby mobile OS suppliers are more strictly regulated than PC OS suppliers.

paragraphs.text: PCs and PC browsers play a critical role. It would be nonsensical and a missed opportunity to allow a regulatory gap to emerge whereby mobile OS suppliers are more strictly regulated than PC OS suppliers.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

paragraphs.text: The practices of Microsoft directly impact the daily life of hundreds of millions of EU consumers.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns employed on PCs

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns employed on PCs interfere with, undermine and do not respect consumer choice and should be treated as what they are: unfair practices. This must include the right to freely download and set as default the consumer's preferred browser and for that choice to be fully respected, i.e., without unnecessary obstruction or interference.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

paragraphs.text: The BCA, and its member companies, stands ready to assist the Commission further and is grateful that the Commission considers these issues in its enforcement as well as in the creation of any potential new legislative framework.

section_path: III. Removing dark patterns on PC devices within the Single Market is a necessary step to enhance consumer choice

evidence_quotes.quote: disproportionate and misleading scare screen message

paragraphs.text: Figure 4: Complex process to exit S mode, followed by disproportionate and misleading scare screen message

"You may want to think twice about switching out of S mode. Especially if performance and peace of mind are your top priorities. S mode was designed for security, speed, and simplicity – offering thousands of Microsoft-verified apps and Microsoft Edge, the fast and safe web browser. Please note you will not be able to return to S mode once you've switched."
